items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
items.correct: Its tertiary structure (3D shape) changes, particularly the active site, so it can no longer bind substrate effectively.
items.misconception: The enzyme has died because enzymes are alive.
items.random_wrong: The enzyme has been completely broken into amino acids.
items.why: Students think of enzymes as alive (probably because they are produced by living things), so they describe denaturation as death rather than shape change.

items.q: Why are enzymes specific to certain substrates?
items.correct: Only substrates with complementary shape and chemistry to the active site can bind effectively.
items.misconception: Each enzyme can react with any substrate, but works fastest with one type.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes choose their substrate based on which one is most common in the cell.
items.why: Students underestimate how strict the lock-and-key/induced-fit relationship is; they think specificity is a preference rather than a structural requirement.

items.q: Is an enzyme used up during the reaction it speeds up?
items.correct: No — an enzyme is a catalyst and is not used up; it can catalyse the same reaction repeatedly.
items.misconception: Enzymes are consumed in the reactions they catalyse.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes are living and die after one use.
items.why: Students treat enzymes as reactants, but as catalysts they emerge unchanged and remain available for further reactions.

items.q: Do prokaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus?
items.correct: No — prokaryotes have no nucleus; their DNA floats free in the cytoplasm. Only eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus.
items.misconception: All cells have a nucleus surrounded by a membrane.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes have no DNA at all.
items.why: Students assume every cell is eukaryote-like, but prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles and store their DNA loose in the cytoplasm.

items.q: Which organelle holds the DNA in a eukaryote?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Nucleus. “Mitochondrion” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which structure do plant cells have that animal cells do NOT?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cell wall. “Cell membrane” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Maximum useful magnification of a school light microscope:
items.why: The real figure is 2000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
